These videos have been performed by Professor Tristan Yan at the Royal Prince Alfred Hospital in Sydney and we're using the posterior approach where the surgeon stands at the back of the patient, the camera is just posterior to the inferior border of the scapula and then there are two lower down ports, one more anterior, one more posterior and you can see Two Roberts with peanuts here, just coming from those lower ports. So some really important things to say. Firstly, this is not sped up. This is not edited. This is real time, the whole lobectomy. So actually, posterior approach can be a very fast technique. The other useful thing for watching this video for is, let's say you're doing redo surgery and want to look for a new technique. If you go posterior having gone anterior, then you're going to have far fewer adhesions and be able to take them down. Also, robotics is much more of an either posterior or inferior technique in the view. is very similar to what you're going to see in the video here so let's get cracking so we've got an absolutely wonderful fissure here so on the top of the screen is an upper lobe and the lower lobe is at the bottom of the screen and we're just gently using peanuts to brush away the lung off the pulmonary artery.

The surgeon uses a blunt tip dissector to carefully dissect around the posterior segmental artery, visible in the center of the view. The bronchus is located just anterior to the dissector in the surgeon's left hand. The surgeon employs gentle blunt dissection to separate the artery from surrounding tissues, pushing until resistance is felt and then separating the tissue planes.

The surgeon gently separates tissue around the posterior segmental artery using blunt dissection. The tips of the instruments are visible as they encircle the artery. A Cavidian Tan-45 stapler is introduced through an anterior utility incision, positioned along the fissure line to staple the artery. The camera view is posterior, utilizing either a ten or five millimeter camera.

The surgeon uses a metal-tipped sucker to clean the area and perform dissection around a silicotic lymph node near the pulmonary artery. Blunt instruments are employed to gently push the lymph node off the artery, avoiding damage. The pulmonary artery sheath is exposed, ensuring the lymph node does not invade it. The Apollo bronchus is visible to the left of the lymph node, with the apical segmental artery nearby.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to separate tissues around the pulmonary artery, revealing the tissue plane within the sheath. The posterior side of the vessel is exposed, with visible pleura and soft tissue. Diathermy is applied to clear the view of the apical segmental artery. The aorta is visible, distant from the recurrent laryngeal nerve and vagus nerve. The bronchus is seen to the left, with the lingular artery further left. Minimal diathermy is used, relying on blunt dissection and peanuts.

The surgeon navigates around the apical segmental artery, successfully bypassing a silicotic lymph node using tactile and visual feedback. A straight dissector is used to position a Tan-45 stapler without articulation, ensuring no tension on the vessel or lung during firing. The lymph node is dissected out, and the anterior segmental artery is exposed amidst soft tissue. The surgeon examines the lingular artery and anterior oblique fissure, maintaining a dry surgical field.

The surgeon lifts the anterior oblique fissure, using a dissector to carefully expose the area anterior to the lingula. The tips of the instruments are visible, and a stapler is prepared for use on the thin tissue of the anterior oblique fissure. The lingular artery is clearly visible, with a thin, glassy sheath resembling cellophane covering it. The surgeon gently separates lymph nodes from the artery without poking, ensuring a safe dissection.

The surgeon staples the lingular artery, then focuses on dissecting around the anterior segmental artery (A-one), bronchus, and vein. Using a posterior approach, the surgeon carefully separates lymph nodes and tissue planes to expose the pulmonary artery and bronchus. The surgeon uses tactile feedback to ensure the instrument tips are correctly positioned, avoiding poking and instead gently separating tissues to safely navigate around the bronchus and vein.

The surgeon performs the crane maneuver by lifting the lower lobe bronchus with a vessel loop, ensuring a clear track for stapling away from the pulmonary artery. After multiple separations, the bronchus is stapled using purple automatic staples. The anterior segmental artery is dissected out, with careful separation from the main pulmonary artery, while avoiding excessive poking to prevent damage.

The surgeon uses a tan stapler to staple straight up the fissure, taking advantage of the widened intercostal space for less pain. The vein is not dissected out, as the stapler is positioned above the pulmonary artery, ensuring no risk of hitting it. The stapler is applied around the hilum, targeting the vein and bronchus, with a purple sixty stapler used for completion. A lymph node is visible, which will be removed during the lymphadenectomy.
